importNotes: Draft claim candidates derived from evidence-module artifacts. No claim in this file has been reviewed or promoted. All carry reviewStatus: draft and scoreImpact: candidate or none. Promotion requires passing PPW claim-promotion gates; no child-repository support rating is automatically promoted.

draftClaims.claim: Lincoln's rhetoric constructs the Civil War as a test that must be endured rather than won — an obligatory frame in which the nation has no choice but to pass through suffering to prove its founding promise. This frame appears across formal addresses (Gettysburg, Second Inaugural), private letters (to Hodges, 1864), and annual messages, suggesting a stable cognitive structure rather than an occasional rhetorical device.
limitations: Passage-level anchoring not yet completed; claim rests on document-level corpus inventory, not imported passage records
Cross-register frequency not quantified; anecdotal cross-genre appearance cited but not systematically measured
Obligatory-frame definition may overlap with sacrifice cluster; conceptual boundary requires review-gate clarification
promotionBlockers: Passage records must be imported (PPW #143) and claim anchored to specific passages
Inter-annotator reliability check required for cluster-membership judgment
Claim-promotion gate not yet designed (PPW #129, Milestone 18)

draftClaims.claim: Lincoln consistently uses organic body metaphors — bleeding wounds, healing, the sick body politic — to frame the Union not as a legal compact but as a living organism whose injury and recovery are morally legible. This framing intensifies after 1862 and reaches its apex in the Second Inaugural's 'every drop of blood' passage, structuring the war's meaning as medical rather than punitive.
limitations: Passage-level anchoring not yet completed
Diachronic intensification claim (stronger after 1862) requires quantitative evidence from full passage import
Organic metaphor may be conventional political rhetoric of the period; comparative baseline against contemporaries (Douglas, Chase) not yet established
promotionBlockers: Passage records must be imported (PPW #143)
Contemporaneous-context comparison documents needed (PPW #197 reference tier)
Claim-promotion gate not yet designed (PPW #129)

draftClaims.claim: In Lincoln's Peoria Speech and subsequent antislavery addresses, the covenant with the Founders' principles (all men created equal) functions as the supreme moral authority over both the legal institution of slavery and the practical demands of war. Freedom is framed not as a natural right asserted from below but as a promise owed upward to a founding covenant — a structure that constrains the moral logic of emancipation.
limitations: Passage-level anchoring not yet completed
Relationship between covenant-over-freedom cluster and obligatory-frame cluster is not yet formally distinguished; theoretical boundary requires review-gate adjudication

Emancipation Proclamation (lma-doc-015/016) explicitly suppresses covenant rhetoric in favor of military necessity; the cluster's absence in that document is a negative finding that should be incorporated
promotionBlockers: Passage records must be imported (PPW #143)
Cluster boundary definition requires inter-annotator reliability check
Claim-promotion gate not yet designed (PPW #129)

draftClaims.claim: Lincoln's sacrifice cluster in the Gettysburg Address reframes soldiers' deaths as generative rather than tragic: the dead do not merely die for the Union but give birth to a new nation. This generative sacrifice logic — distinguishing Lincoln's rhetoric from pure martyrology — is absent from the Emancipation Proclamations, suggesting it is register-specific (formal civic address) rather than a universal feature of his moral vocabulary.
limitations: Passage-level anchoring not yet completed
Register-specificity claim (formal civic address vs. legal proclamation) requires systematic cross-register analysis across the full corpus
Generative vs. tragic sacrifice distinction requires precise operationalisation before it can be reliably coded by independent annotators
promotionBlockers: Passage records must be imported (PPW #143)
Cross-register corpus coverage requires validation-tier documents (PPW #196, pending LMA #114)
Claim-promotion gate not yet designed (PPW #129)

draftClaims.claim: The Sacrifice Law comparative corpus is suitable for testing whether sacred-political orders under perceived existential pressure generate sacrificial rhetoric across multiple political settings, but the current PPW evidence state supports only a draft comparative candidate, not a public support rating.
limitations: Candidate is based on corpus-level and artifact-family metadata, not imported passage records
American Revolution and Napoleonic France now have PPW case folders and reference-only subset links, but no Sacrifice Law passage records are imported for those subsets
No reliability sampling design has been adapted to PPW for this corpus
No child-repository public support rating is promoted by this draft candidate

Add passage-level evidence for every included case target before review or promotion
Complete reliability and claim-promotion review gates before any public finding

draftClaims.claim: The Lincoln subset of the Sacrifice Law comparative corpus may support a draft contrast between bounded civic sacrifice and more pathological sacrifice patterns, but PPW should keep this separate from the lincoln-deep-28 mechanism-discovery corpus until passage-level evidence and review gates are in place.
limitations: The Sacrifice Law Lincoln subset is comparative and does not replace lincoln-deep-28
No Sacrifice Law passage records have been imported into PPW for this candidate
The bounded-vs-pathological contrast requires operational review before scoring impact can be assessed

Complete claim-promotion gate review before any score impact is applied
